name: Meretseger
description: Meretseger, whose name means 'She Who Loves Silence,' was a cobra-headed or woman-headed deity in ancient Egyptian mythology, primarily associated with the Theban Necropolis on the west bank of the Nile, opposite Thebes (modern Luxor). She was the protector of the Valley of the Kings, Valley of the Queens, and the workers and artisans who built and decorated the royal tombs. Meretseger's role was to guard against tomb robbers and punish those who desecrated the sacred burial sites. Her power lay in her ability to inflict illness and blindness upon those who wronged her, but she was also known for her compassion and healing abilities towards those who repented and sought forgiveness. She was a powerful force, revered and feared in equal measure, embodying the delicate balance between justice and mercy within the ancient Egyptian worldview. She was often depicted as a cobra, a symbol of protection and venomous retribution, or as a woman with the head of a cobra, signifying her dual nature. Offerings were often left at her shrines by workers seeking her protection and forgiveness for any unintentional transgressions. She was a vital aspect of the religious landscape of Thebes, ensuring the sanctity of the afterlife for the pharaohs and their consorts.
subtitle: Silent Guardian of the Theban Necropolis

keyMyths.title: Meretseger's Wrath
keyMyths.description: A worker stole from a tomb and was struck blind. He repented and prayed to Meretseger, and she restored his sight as a sign of her mercy.
keyMyths.source: Based on inscriptions from Deir el-Medina

keyMyths.title: Protection of the Tombs
keyMyths.description: Meretseger guarded the tombs from robbers, using her venomous bite to punish those who dared to disturb the resting place of the pharaohs.
keyMyths.source: Interpretations of tomb decorations and inscriptions

keyMyths.title: The Builder's Plea
keyMyths.description: The artisans building the tombs would pray to Meretseger for protection from accidents and illnesses, seeking her favor in their dangerous work.

sources.author: Hart, George
sources.work: A Dictionary of Egyptian Gods and Goddesses

sources.author: Wilkinson, Richard H.
sources.work: The Complete Gods and Goddesses of Ancient Egypt

sources.text: Detailed descriptions of various deities, including Meretseger.
